ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Aloo Bonda; mapped to the closest parent class 'fritter (food product)' as Aloo Bonda is a deep-fried battered food product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Aloo Bonda; mapped to 'Potato products and dishes' (A0CKP) as the closest defensible category given the primary ingredient is potato.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Aloo Bonda or equivalent fried potato fritter dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Aloo Bonda is 2–3 pieces (approximately 150 g), commonly served as a snack or appetizer alongside mint chutney or tamarind sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Aloo Bonda is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried potato-based snacks (Batata Vada / Aloo Bonda equivalent) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for potato fritters (FDC ID 173944). Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with both sources. Minor micronutrient values (calcium, iron, vitamin C) are drawn from ICMR-NIN potato and chickpea flour composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Bonda. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the analogous food 'potato, boiled, mashed' (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted for the chickpea flour batter coating (which has a lower GI of ~35) and deep-frying. The net effect of deep-frying and the starchy potato filling dominates, placing the composite GI in the high range (~70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 32.0) / 100 = 22.4, using 150 g serving with 21.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g (= 32.0 g per 150 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g combined with high caloric density from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Aloo Bonda to occasional small portions (1 piece) and request reduced-salt preparation, as the 300 mg sodium per 100 g combined with the high fat load from deep-frying contributes to elevated cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) from starchy potato filling with a glycemic load of 22.4 per 150 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aloo Bonda is unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~70) and high glycemic load (22.4 per serving); if consumed, portion should be limited to one piece paired with a high-fibre accompaniment such as mint chutney with yogurt to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14.7 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.7 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Bonda is high in total fat and contains trans fat from deep-frying, both of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should avoid regular consumption and consider baked or air-fried alternatives to reduce fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; potato and green chilli are nightshade ingredients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Aloo Bonda due to AGE formation from deep-frying and the presence of nightshade ingredients (potato, green chilli) that may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Bonda is a popular Indian deep-fried snack consisting of a spiced mashed potato filling encased in a chickpea flour (besan) batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The filling typically contains boiled and mashed potatoes seasoned with mustard seeds, turmeric, green chillies, curry leaves, fresh coriander, and salt. The outer batter is made from chickpea flour mixed with water, turmeric, and salt, creating a crunchy shell that contrasts with the soft, flavourful interior. Aloo Bonda is closely related to Batata Vada, a popular Mumbai street food, and is widely served across South and West India as a tea-time snack or appetizer. In Singapore, it is commonly found in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, often served with mint chutney or tamarind sauce. Nutritionally, Aloo Bonda is energy-dense at approximately 233 kcal per 100 g, with 14.7 g fat, 21.3 g carbohydrate, and 5.0 g protein per 100 g. The high glycemic index (~70) and glycemic load (~22 per 150 g serving) make it unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The sodium content of 300 mg per 100 g warrants caution for those with hypertension. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Aloo Bonda?
Is Aloo Bonda suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Aloo Bonda and Batata Vada?
How much fat is in Aloo Bonda?
What is the glycemic index of Aloo Bonda?
Can I eat Aloo Bonda if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Aloo Bonda is not listed as a discrete entry in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for equivalent deep-fried potato-based snacks and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for potato fritters. The GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled/mashed potato. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Aloo Bonda is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a discrete entry; values could not be reliably disaggregated from the composite frying oil contribution. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation is not reported in the adapted ICMR-NIN source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific preparation in the adapted ICMR-NIN or USDA sources consulted. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: Aloo Bonda is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is confirmed zero. trans_fat_g of 0.3 g/100 g reflects trace trans fat from commercial deep-frying oil as reported in analogous fried snack data. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Dietary Guidelines URL) was retained as a supporting reference but the primary nutrient adaptation used the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods publication and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The ethnic_tags 'Curry' and 'Naan' from the input were removed as they are not applicable to Aloo Bonda. country_of_origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'India' as Aloo Bonda originates in India; it is served in Singapore but is not a Singaporean dish. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore's Indian food ecosystem.
